content:
4 min read
Just now

--

At some point, AI systems stop looking like chatbots and start looking a lot more like the distributed systems many of us have been building for years.
If you’ve ever supported a production environment, you know that problems don’t wait for someone to open a chat window.

• A pipeline fails overnight.

• A developer submits a pull request.

• A security scanner finds a vulnerability.

• A data quality check fails.

• A support ticket comes in.

• Something breaks in an upstream system.
The events keep coming, whether anyone is sitting at a keyboard or not.
This is where proactive loops start to get interesting.
From Waiting for Prompts to Responding to Events
Most of the AI tools we use today start with a person.
We ask a question or give the system a task, and it responds.
A proactive loop works differently. Something happens in the environment, and that event starts the work.
The basic pattern looks something like this:
Event → Route → Work → Review → Act
An event comes in. The system figures out what kind of problem it is, sends it to the right agent or workflow, reviews the result, and decides what should happen…
